Back: Strict Mode is a development-only wrapper that highlights unsafe lifecycle patterns, deprecated APIs, and side-effect issues.
Why: It helps surface bugs early so components remain safe under concurrent rendering behavior.

Front: Why does React Strict Mode double-render in development?
Back: React intentionally invokes render-related paths more than once in development to expose impure rendering side effects.
Why: This catches hidden mutations and non-idempotent logic before production, where they are harder to diagnose.

Back: Suspense coordinates async UI by letting components pause rendering and show fallback content until data or code is ready.
Why: It centralizes loading boundaries, improving perceived performance and reducing ad-hoc loading state wiring.

Back: Ivy is Angular's rendering and compilation engine that produces smaller bundles and faster builds.
Why: Its tree-shaking and locality improvements reduce runtime cost and improve developer iteration speed.

Back: An observable that can multicast values to multiple subscribers.
Why: Useful for events and shared streams.

Back: The JVM is the runtime that executes Java bytecode and provides memory management, JIT compilation, and portability.
Why: It lets Java applications run consistently across operating systems with performance optimizations at runtime.

Back: Thread safety means shared state remains correct when accessed concurrently by multiple threads.
Why: Without it, race conditions and visibility bugs can corrupt data and cause intermittent failures.

Back: Auto-configuration creates sensible Spring bean setup based on classpath dependencies and application properties.
Why: It removes repetitive boilerplate while still allowing explicit overrides when needed.

Back: Spring Actuator exposes operational endpoints and metrics for health, readiness, tracing, and runtime insight.
Why: These signals are critical for monitoring, alerting, and safe production operations.

Back: CQRS separates write models (commands) from read models (queries) so each side can be optimized independently.
Why: It helps when read/write access patterns differ greatly, but it increases architectural complexity.

Back: Event sourcing stores state changes as an append-only event log instead of only persisting current state.
Why: It provides auditability and replay, but requires careful schema evolution and projection management.

Back: Blue-green deployment runs old and new production environments in parallel, then switches traffic to the new version.
Why: It reduces release risk and enables fast rollback by switching traffic back if issues appear.

Back: SQL injection is an attack where untrusted input is interpreted as query syntax and changes intended SQL behavior.
Why: Parameterized queries and strict validation are essential to prevent data leakage, tampering, and privilege abuse.

Back: OAuth is a delegated authorization framework that lets users grant scoped access without sharing passwords.
Why: It enables secure third-party integrations with revocable, least-privilege access tokens.

Back: CORS is a browser security mechanism that controls cross-origin HTTP requests based on server policy headers.
Why: Configured correctly, it enables safe frontend-backend communication across different origins.
